title: Influencing Control: Jawboning in Risk Arbitrage
abstract: In an “activist risk arbitrage,” a shareholder attempts to improve terms of an announced M&A through public campaigns. Activists target deals with low premiums and those susceptible to managerial conflicts of interest, including going-private deals and deals in which CEOs receive outsized payments. Activist arbitrageurs are associated with a significant decrease in the probability that targets will be sold to the announced bidders, and an increase in the premium paid, both ex post among surviving deals and ex ante among all deals. Activist arbitrage serves as a governance mechanism in M&A and earns higher returns than passive arbitrage.

article.introduction:
In an “activist risk arbitrage,” a shareholderwho often acquires a significant stake in a target company after an announced takeover attempts to block the announced deal under its current terms. The dissident shareholder threatens to vote against the deal and conducts public campaigns designed to persuade other shareholders to do the same unless the target company's management team is able to improve the deal premium. In the presence of such activists, deals are more likely to fail, but the average premium paid is higher even taking incremental deal failure into account. This strategy, as its name suggests, combines shareholder activism with a more traditional, “passive” risk arbitrage approach. In particular, it serves both as a governance remedy for shareholders who deem announced merger and acquisition (M&A) deals approved by management/boards as inadequate, and as an investment strategy for specialized investors, especially hedge funds.
Activist risk arbitrage is a relatively new and understudied form of shareholder activism. While activist arbitrageurs were virtually nonexistent prior to 2000, they challenged about 10% of the proposed M&A deals in the mid-2000s, with the rate stabilizing to about 5% toward the end of our sample period. In general, institutional investor activism seeks to influence corporate policies to bring about operational, financial, and governance improvements, but does not aim for control (Brav et al. (2008a)). However, when an investor launches an activism campaign in an attempt to change the course of an M&A deal in process, the investor is trying to exert control. In this paper, we use a comprehensive sample of 277 activist arbitrage events among over 3,000 M&A deals from 2000 to 2015 to study the determinants, motives, and consequences of this new form of activism, and to analyze its interaction with the market for corporate control.
Activist arbitrageurs choose their targets strategically. The probability of activist involvement increases 3.2 percentage points (relative to the unconditional probability of 6%) with every 1 standard deviation decrease in announcement premium, controlling for deal characteristics. This result is consistent with the commonly declared motive for dissent, namely, inadequate payment. Activists are significantly more likely to disturb otherwise “friendly” deals, especially going-private deals in which management is part of or affiliated with the acquirers (with an incremental 3.1 percentage point increase in the probability of targeting). Presumably, the concern with such deals is that a company's board and management team, having endorsed favored acquirers, may have failed to perform due diligence by extracting optimal terms from the acquirers or by soliciting competing bids.
Further tests support the governance motive for activist targeting. First, we show that the odds of activist targeting increase more than 60% when a merger agreement offers special severance payments to the target's top management team contingent on successful deal completion, as this creates a conflict of interest whereby top management benefits disproportionately from selling the company to a particular acquirer. M&A deals in which CEOs receive “excessive” compensation, a common proxy for poor governance, are also associated with increased risk of activist intervention. These results suggest that activist risk arbitrage is a potentially important governance mechanism that can be used to protect investors' interests in corporate control changes susceptible to management self-dealing or other managerial conflicts of interest.
Activists' attempts to block a deal, or to pressure management into extracting higher prices from a current buyer or soliciting multiple bids, are associated with outcomes consistent with their means and goals. We find that when activists are involved, the probability of deal completion, that is, of a target being sold to an announced bidder, is 5.8 percentage points lower (significant at the 5% level) relative to the full-sample completion rate of 83.3%. When we look into the “black box” of each failed deal using information from corporate filings, press releases, and news searches, we find that activists likely played a role in about two-thirds of the failed deals in which they were present. This occurs, for example, when their negative votes turn out to be pivotal or a firm is eventually sold to an activist-recommended third party.
Because they tend to hold minority stakes (typically 5% to 10%), activists need to influence and persuade fellow shareholders to achieve their goals. Their tactics, such as press releases that criticize management/deals and proxy solicitations that propose alternatives to current deals, share commonalities with general activist strategies aimed at operational or governance changes. Because an M&A deal needs a majority (or supermajority) shareholder vote to be passed, activists' relationship with proxy advisory firms such as Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) is of particular interest because the latter exerts considerable influence on the voting behavior of their institutional investor clients (Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch (2013), Malenko and Shen (2016)). We find that ISS virtually never issues a recommendation against a deal unless there is public dissension by shareholders. Moreover, such negative recommendations, which comprise 28% of ISS recommendations in our sample, are invariably issued following activist announcements (with a median delay of 48 days). A negative ISS recommendation is associated with a 21 percentage point decrease in the probability of deal completion. Taken together, these results suggest that ISS serves as an important proxy voting intermediary for activism against an M&A deal.
Nevertheless, shareholders of target companies, including activists themselves, are better off following completed rather than failed deals because stock prices often revert to preannouncement levels after a deal is called off. This implies that, for the activist arbitrage strategy to be viable, incremental deal failure needs to be more than offset by a positive premium revision in surviving deals. It is thus good news, for outside shareholders, that the presence of activists is associated with a premium revision of 3.4 percentage points across all deals (including failed ones), or 4.3 percentage points conditional on deal completion. Moreover, only minor delays (of about 10 to 14 days) occur in activist-involved deals between the announcement date and deal completion. It is also reassuring, for acquirers, that activism on the opposite side does not leave acquirer shareholders worse off relative to similar deals in the M&A market: average abnormal stock returns for acquirers are actually higher (albeit insignificantly so) than matched deals during the period from just before deal announcement to deal resolution.
In sum, activist arbitrageurs earn significant positive returns after adjusting for common risk factors, and they earn overall higher average returns than passive risk arbitrageurs, which compensates for “jaw pain” as well as for the assumption of higher deal risk. The four-factor adjusted cumulative abnormal returns (CARs) for activists amount to 4.9% to 5.4% per deal, compared to 2.2% for passive arbitrageurs matched to the same time window. The calendar-time portfolio approach yields a monthly four-factor alpha of 1.60% compared to 0.51% for the passive arbitrage strategy (the difference is significant at the 5% level). Finally, the market overall welcomes the presence of activists, with an average appreciation in target company share price of 2.4% upon an activist announcement. Therefore, activists earn good returns for themselves, and buy-and-hold shareholders share in the gains from improved deal terms.
In addition to characterizing a form of shareholder activism in the setting of a takeover, our study highlights a novel governance remedy in M&As. A vast literature pioneered by Grossman and Hart (1980) and Fama (1980) views takeovers as the ultimate market-based antidote to managerial agency problems because poor performance due to managerial agency makes ripe targets for takeovers. Takeovers are, therefore, beneficial on average for target shareholders who typically receive most of the total surplus (Eckbo and Thorburn (2013)). Accordingly, a misalignment between management and outside shareholders often manifests as a management defense against takeover deals desired by shareholders, reflecting the former's private benefits of control.
In our setting, in contrast, management is eager to sell, but at terms deemed inadequate by some shareholders, which leads to dissension. Such misalignment could be due to managerial conflicts of interest, such as affiliation with an acquirer or deal-specific “golden parachutes.” In the absence of effective coordination, there is little recourse for shareholders to remedy the situation once a deal is approved by management and a company's board. The collective action problem is particularly pertinent in the context of going-private deals and minority freeze-out (DeAngelo, DeAngelo, and Rice (1984)). Activists serve as coordinators but can succeed only by winning majority support from fellow shareholders. Thus, gains accruing to other shareholders through positive premium revision are an essential outcome associated with an activist endeavor.
The role played by activist arbitrageurs is also distinct from other forms of shareholder monitoring in the M&A process. Fich, Harford, and Tran (2015) find that the presence of “motivated” long-term institutional investors is associated with a lower probability of receiving a bid but a higher probability of completion, suggesting that the presence of motivated incumbent shareholders is associated with higher quality deals. In contract, activist arbitrageurs aim to improve lower quality deals. Boyson, Gantchev, and Shivdasani (2017) analyze an alternative strategy whereby activists target firms that are “sellable” and facilitate such transactions. Their activists establish toeholds and announce their intentions before M&A deals are announced. In our setting, activists emerge against announced deals, which reverses predictions pertaining to the relationship between activist presence and deal completion. Activist risk arbitrageurs thus play a unique role in protecting shareholder interests in corporate control.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes our data and summary statistics. In SectionII, we investigate the determinants of activist arbitrageurs' participation in M&A deals. In SectionIII, we relate activists' participation to deal resolution. In SectionIV, we analyze the return on this investment strategy. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Data Sources and Sample Overview:
Our sample of M&As announced between January 1, 2000 and December 31, 2015 is constructed using the Securities Data Company (SDC) database. We include all attempted acquisitions, whether they are completed, applying the following filters commonly used in prior M&A literature (Hsieh and Walkling (2005), Gaspar, Massa, and Matos (2005), Baker and Savasoglu (2002)): (1) the target company must be covered by CRSP before a deal announcement; (2) the acquirer must own less than 50% of the target's stock before the acquisition and more than 50% after the acquisition; (3) each deal must be classified as a stock, cash, or hybrid (part stock and part cash) deal;1and (4) the transaction must not be classified by the SDC as a divestiture, spin-off, or repurchase. These criteria result in an initial sample of 4,547 deals. Actual sample size varies across our analyses depending on the availability of additional required information.
Finally, we verify in the Factiva news archive any merger whose deal status is labeled as “Pending.” If a deal has since been completed or withdrawn, we change its status accordingly. All deals in our sample reached resolution as of June 2017. We also verify the status of each nonpending deal using the Factiva news archive and correct any misclassification. Variables indicating, for each deal, the announcement date, effective date, withdrawal date, deal premium, and target and acquirer characteristics come from the SDC. Institutional holdings data come from the Thomson Financial 13F Database, and firm characteristics as well as stock prices/returns come from Compustat and CRSP, respectively.
Activist risk arbitrage is a relatively new phenomenon that lacks a widely accepted definition. The following case illustrates the general process. In December 2012, Plains Exploration & Production (NYSE ticker: PXP), a petroleum company based in Houston, was preparing to be acquired by Freeport-McMoRan (NYSE ticker: FCX), a natural resources company based in Phoenix. At the offer price of $45.96, existing shareholders stood to gain a premium of 26.2% over the preannouncement price. A special meeting to approve the merger was scheduled for May 20, 2013. On May 6, 2013, CR Intrinsic Investors, a subsidiary of SAC Capital Advisors with a 3.8% ownership share of PXP, sent a public letter to the board announcing its intention to vote against the deal and to persuade other shareholders to do the same. The letter stated that CR Intrinsic valued PXP at $49.56 based on the company's strong results and industry performance following the merger agreement.
By then a “wolf pack,” a group of informally coordinated investors, appeared to have formed to oppose the merger. On the same day, Arrowgrass Capital Partners, a hedge fund based in London and New York, announced a 3.7% stake in PXP and denounced the proposed merger. John Paulson, another hedge fund manager who was the largest outside shareholder (9.9%) at the time, did not express his voting preference. The dissidents quickly secured support from the two leading proxy advisors, namely, ISS and Glass, Lewis & Co., both of which recommended voting against the transaction on the following day. At the special meeting on May 20, FCX allowed PXP to declare a special one-time dividend of $3 per share prior to merger consummation and promised supplemental postmerger dividends. Paulson immediately pledged his shares in favor of the deal, and the merger proposal passed at the meeting held later that day. The stock closed at $48.99, for a 38.2% premium over the pre-announcement price. During the same period, the S&P 500 appreciated 16.8% and the energy sector index (NYSE: VDE) rose 14.2%.
“Risk arbitrage,” whereby a speculator takes a long position in a target company immediately following an acquisition announcement in hopes of profiting from full price convergence at deal consummation, has long been adopted as a strategy in the M&A market. In such a “passive” arbitrage, a speculator does not “voice” his opinion other than by voting his shares. In contrast, CR Intrinsic loudly argued that the target deserved a higher bid and threatened to block the deal via both its own voting rights and, more importantly, its influence on other shareholders. If it had adopted a passive risk arbitrage strategy and the deal went through as planned, CR Intrinsic would have earned a return of 3.3% on its long position (from immediately after the initial merger announcement to the final tendering of the stock at $45.96). Instead, CR Intrinsic pocketed a much higher return of 10.1% due to the revised premium. Its incremental costs were the time/effort spent in jawboning and in writing and disseminating public letters, and the increased risk that the deal would fall through completely, after which the price could revert to its pre-announcement level.
Figure1illustrates the typical path of an activist arbitrage in a merger target from deal announcement to resolution, juxtaposed with that of a conventional passive arbitrage.
Loosely speaking, an activist arbitrage can be any attempt by target shareholders to profit from an announced M&A deal by exercising shareholder rights beyond voting. The primary data source that we use to identify all activist arbitrage events is SharkRepellent, a data provider specializing in corporate governance that identifies 244 merger targets with activist campaigns (334 deal-activist pairs) during the period 2000 to 2015 under the category “Vote/Activism Against a Merger.”2For each target firm, we identify as activist arbitrageurs those investors who publicly criticized a transaction or solicited proxies against a deal. Of the 281 unique investors, 230 are hedge funds, 14 are other institutional investors, 15 are companies, and the remaining 22 are individuals.3We then manually collect activist arbitrageurs' plans and actions through their press releases (letters to boards/management teams) and Schedule 13D filings if these investors acquired more than 5% of a publicly traded target company. Such information includes the ownership stake, announcement date (press release or Schedule 13D filing date), and, if a campaign was unsuccessful, withdrawal date.
Additional steps ensure sample completeness. First, we manually collect all Schedule 13D filings between deal announcement and deal resolution for all mergers announced during the sample period. Second, a filing entity is classified as an activist arbitrageur if it satisfies either of the following two criteria: (1) the entity states under Item 4 that the purpose of its investment was to object to the current structure of the acquisition, or to propose alternative terms for the deal,4and (2) results of our extensive news searches in Factiva yield press releases (letters to boards/management teams) indicating that an activist expressed concerns about an announced deal and objected to an acquisition under the current contract terms. The additional Schedule 13D search increases the number of merger targets with activist campaigns to 277.
Figure2plots the frequency of merger transactions and activist arbitrage activities on the target side over our sample period. Activist arbitrage activity is generally correlated with M&A volumes, reaching its peak in 2007 before dropping significantly during the financial crisis and then stabilizing in the following years. The full-sample probability of activist targeting is 6%. This relatively modest frequency is not surprising given that takeovers are generally considered beneficial to target shareholders because target shareholders receive most of the joint surplus of a merger (Eckbo and Thorburn (2013)).
The top four activists in our sample are GAMCO Investors, Inc., Ramius LLC, Millennium Management LLC, and Elliott Associates, LP. Combined, these firms account for about 9.4% of all deals in our sample. It is worth noting that there is some overlap between activist and passive players in risk arbitrage. About 18.5% of the passive arbitrageurs in our sample launched at least one activist campaign.
The ownership share is sizable but represents strictly minority stakes; it is comparable to what is observed in general hedge fund activism as reported in the literature. Several factors constrain the size of individual activists' ownership share, including single-position risk limits, the trade-off between the potential for value improvements and price impact (Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015,2016)), and mandated disclosures such that ownership above 5% has to be acquired at a price that incorporates potential value improvement.5
The most common “influence”-based tactics are as follows: (1) Public criticism of the transaction through letters addressed to the target's board and/or shareholders, usually accompanied by press releases (158 deals). These letters are often attached to Schedule 13D filings under Item 4 (169 deals). (2) Proxy solicitation intended to veto the deal (53 deals, 24 of which involve proxy contests). (3) Proposing alternative acquisitions (13 deals). (4) Lobbying proxy advisory firms like ISS in order to influence their institutional shareholder clients.6These tactics are similar to those documented in the general hedge fund activism literature, except that they involve a more focused effort to influence shareholder votes.
“Wolf packs,” that is, informally coordinated investors in a target firm that work together to support a common agenda, are not uncommon in activist arbitrage.7In 28.6% of our activist sample deals, we are able to identify at least two publicly announced activists. They typically disclose their intentions within several weeks of each other, often within a few days, and in most such cases the “follower” explicitly supports the claim of the “leader” that the deal terms are unfair to shareholders. This percentage is likely to underestimate the presence of “wolf packs” because the coordination may not be disclosed, or may even just take the form of mutually consistent beliefs, as analyzed in Brav, Dasgupta, and Mathews (2015). Unless otherwise specified, our analyses are aggregated at the deal level if multiple activists are involved.
Of the 277 deals involving activists in our sample, only 150 (54.2%) are completed under their original terms. In 87 deals (31.4%), target shareholders gained better terms from either the original or new acquirers. The remainder of the deals were withdrawn by either the acquirer or the target. In contrast, 72.0% of the deals in our control sample were completed under the originally stated terms.
Passive risk arbitrageurs are investors who purchase stocks after an acquisition announcement for the purpose of voting on the deal but do not openly criticize or campaign against the deal or attempt to change its major terms. Estimates of total passive arbitrage funds' ownership of a target's shares subsequent to a merger announcement range from 15% during 1992 to 1999 (Hsieh and Walkling (2005)) to 35% during 1985 to 2004 (Officer (2007)). To identify passive arbitrageurs, we follow the methodology developed by Hsieh and Walkling (2005), based on Thomson Reuters's 13F institutional ownership information.
In constructing our sample of passive risk arbitrageurs, we first require that deal announcement and resolution fall in different quarters. This step, which leads us to drop 684 deals that close within the same quarter, ensures that we can calculate the change in institutional ownership around a deal announcement using quarterly holdings data from the 13F database. We further require that an arbitrageur observes a positive change in stock ownership for at least six deals and in more than 60% of all deals in which it discloses holdings between the end of quartert-1 and the end of quartert—presumably the time frame during which the deal is announced. Although the two numerical cutoffs are arbitrary, robustness checks confirm that our main results are not affected by the specific choices made within a reasonable range.
The above steps yield a sample of 329 unique passive risk arbitrageurs between 2000 and 2015. We next identify deals that involve at least one passive arbitrageur but are without activist involvement. The double criteria yield 2,468 deals. In addition to identifying passive risk arbitrageurs through 13F filings, we supplement the search by processing all Schedule 13D filings between announcement and resolution for each deal. A filer is added to the list of passive arbitrageurs if it meets the following two criteria: first, the filing investor does not state under Item 4 of Schedule 13D the purpose of influencing the pending merger beyond exercising its entitled voting rights; second, there is no evidence in the public news archive indicating the opposite. This step yields an additional 249 unique M&A events, and leads to our final sample of 2,717 deals with passive risk arbitrageurs. Compared with activists, the average (median) ownership of a passive risk arbitrageur is much smaller at 0.62% (0.18%), or $14.2 ($2.6) million.
In the other 926 deals, no arbitrageur (passive or activist) involvement is disclosed.
article.II. Deal Selection by Activist Arbitrage:
In our first analysis, reported in Panel A of TableII, we compare the ex ante deal characteristics of merger targets that attract activist arbitrageurs (column (1)) to those of two control samples, namely, merger targets held by passive-only (i.e., traditional) arbitrageurs (column (2)) and targets in deals involving no disclosed arbitrageurs (column (3)). Reflecting the required deal characteristic information, the event sample consists of 220 of 277 activist-involved deals that can be matched to the SDC data. We find that deals held by activist arbitrageurs have an announcement premium (defined as(PInitialOffer−P−1)/P−1, wherePInitialOfferandP−1are the initial offer price and closing price of the target stock on the day prior to the announcement) of 19% on average, compared with 32.6% for deals involving passive arbitrageurs and 38.0% for those without disclosed arbitrageurs. The differences are significant at the 1% level, which suggests that activist arbitrageurs are “bargain hunters” that seek deals with lower announcement premiums. Such deals presumably have more room to sweeten the bid if the low bid stems from a conflict of interest or a lack of bargaining effort on the target side. Indeed, activists are more likely to invest in going-private deals, many of which are management-led buyouts and cash deals, as these financial buyers tend to make lower bids than strategic or corporate buyers (e.g., Bargeron et al. (2008)).
Panel B compares key ex post deal outcomes. Deals involving activist arbitrageurs have on average a higher revision return (defined as the increase in the acquirer's bid scaled by target share price immediately before the initial takeover announcement; the premium revision measure includes special dividends for nine activist-targeted deals and 20 nonevent deals, all of which are collected from definitive merger agreements). In contrast, passive arbitrage is not associated with a significant positive premium revision, which is in line with Hsieh and Walkling (2005) and reflects the defining property of the passive investment strategy. In addition, the probability of multiple bidders for activist-involved deals, at 26.4%, is 19.4 and 14.7 percentage points higher than for the passive-only and no-arbitrage subsamples, respectively, with the differences significant at the 1% level. Of the 58 multiple-bidder deals targeted by activist arbitrageurs, 69.0% of them engaged new bidders (including those proposed by the activists) onlyafterthe arbitrageurs started their campaigns. Further, the average completion rate of the deals involving activists, at 77.2%, is 7.7 and 1.9 percentage points lower than the levels observed in the passive-arbitrage and no-arbitrage subsamples, respectively.
Company filings, disclosures, and news coverage frequently feature activists threatening to block deals under the current terms and at the same time pressuring boards to seek better terms. Such efforts could explain the combination of higher revision returns and a higher frequency of multiple-bidder deals on the one hand and more frequent deal failures on the other hand when activists are present.8Given activists' own voting power and their potential influence over other shareholders, these efforts challenge the target management team to bargain harder on behalf of their shareholders to gain the latter's voting support.
Recent research shows that voting recommendations of leading proxy advisors, particularly ISS, sway up to 25% of the votes in governance matters (Cai, Garner, and Walkling (2009), Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch (2013), Li (2018), Malenko and Shen (2016)). Not surprisingly, extensive evidence from corporate filings and news searches shows that, in addition to trying to reach out to fellow shareholders directly via press conferences, public letters, and proxy solicitations, activists seek endorsement from proxy advisors. The last two rows of Panel B of TableIIshow that ISS issues recommendations in 50.5% of the deals involving activists based on data from proxy filings, press releases, and ISS Voting Analytics.9In the activist-involved cases, ISS always issues recommendations after activist announcements, with a median delay of 48 days, which allows time for activist lobbying. Most informatively, ISS virtually never recommends against a deal without public dissension. Conditional on a recommendation, proxy advisors oppose 27.9% of deals challenged by activists.
TableIIIreports results of predictive regressions in which the dependent variable is a dummy variable for the presence of activists. The set of regressors are the same as those in TableIIwith the critical difference that all variables in the regressions are measured at the time of deal announcement. The sample includes all 3,374 M&A deals for which we have the required information. Column (1) reports the probit coefficients and their associated marginal probabilities. Column (2) reports coefficients from a linear probability model with industry (two-digit SIC) fixed effects. Since the results in the two columns are qualitatively similar, for brevity we focus our discussion on the probit model.
Consistent with the results in TableII, we find that, all else being equal,Announcement premiumhas a negative effect (significant at the 1% level) on the likelihood of an activist arbitrage. A 1 standard deviation increase in announcement premium is associated with a 3.2 percentage point decrease in the marginal probability. Relative to the unconditional probability of an activist arbitrage of 6%, the incremental probability is remarkable. This finding is consistent with the goals of arbitrageurs as commonly stated in their 13D filings or news releases, which often involve key phrases such as “substantially undervalued” and “inadequate.”
The coefficients on additional deal characteristics further support the conflict of interest hypothesis. First, activist arbitrageurs are 3.1 percentage points more likely (significant at the 1% level) to be present inGoing privatedeals. Indeed, going-private deals are overrepresented in the activist event sample: 42.0% of the deals involving activists are going-private deals, while the same proportion among all M&A deals is 25.5%.10The probability of activist intervention also increases by 2.7 percentage points (significant at the 5% level), when a takeover isFriendly(93.3% of all transactions). In a regression framework, these coefficients are net of financial and/or friendly buyers offering lower bids than strategic buyers. The probability of activist involvement further increases, by 41 basis points (significant at the 10% level for the probit model, but not significant after adjusting for industry fixed effects), in respect to a 1 standard deviation increase in acquirer predeal ownership, consistent with Betton and Eckbo's (2000) finding that higher acquirer predeal ownership is associated with terms that are less favorable for targets.
The coefficient onInstitutional ownershipimplies that a 1 standard deviation increase in institutional holdings is associated with a 1.4 percentage point increase in marginal probability (significant at the 1% level), which emphasizes the need for activists to secure the support of institutional investors to overcome the “apathy” of retail and small investors.11In contrast,Insider ownershiphas a negative effect (significant at the 5% level), as such ownership represents pro-incumbent voting power given that the vast majority of deals were endorsed by boards/management teams. The coefficient onROAis also negative (significant at the 5% level), consistent with the finding in the literature that underperformance invites activism.
We find thatDeal valueis not statistically significant, with the economic magnitude of the marginal probability also modest. The nonsignificance ofTarget-acquirer same industrysuggests that activist arbitrageurs focus more on payment terms than on potential synergies.12Finally, none of the variables that characterize deal format—Stock deal,Defense, orTender offer—are associated with activist interest.
Several coefficients (e.g.,Going private) reported in TableIIIsuggest that governance motives underlie activist targeting. In this section, we begin by conducting two tests that more directly characterize activist arbitrage as a governance remedy in M&A. First, we build on Hartzell, Ofek, and Yermack (2004), who show that M&A-triggered severance packages (in the form of special bonuses or sweetened golden parachutes) constitute a major conflict of interest between CEOs (and senior executives) and shareholders, and examine whether such agency-ridden deals are more likely to be targeted by activists. To do so, we manually collect severance payment information from merger-related disclosures before activist arbitrageurs surface.13In particular, we retrieve information on “extra” severance payments in connection with merger agreements, which we define as new severance agreements or amendments to existing agreements with executives because of mergers. We also record cases in which mergers honor preexisting severance plans. The sample for this analysis includes all activist-involved M&A deals and their one-to-one matched deals for firms from the same year-industry with the closest market capitalization.14In TableIV, Panel A, we report results from the regression of activist intervention using the same set of covariates as in TableIII, except with the added regressorsExtra severance payandExisting severance pay.
The results show that extra severance pay increases the likelihood of activist targeting by 66% to 78% significant at the 1% level. This implies an incremental probability of 33.4 or 39.3 percentage points relative to the sample probability of close to 50% by construction, due to the one-to-one matching. In contrast, preexisting severance packages (i.e., not tied to M&As) do not predict activism. When CEOs benefit disproportionally from a takeover compared with outside shareholders, they (and senior executives) are not necessarily in a position to objectively uphold shareholder rights in the negotiation. That activists are more likely to engage targets that grant deal-specific golden parachutes supports the governance motive hypothesis.
Second, we build on Hartzell, Ofek, and Yermack's (2004) finding that special severance packages are significantly correlated with excessive CEO compensation. Indeed, excessive compensation, on its own, is a common proxy for poor governance. Accordingly, we explore the relationship between excess compensation and activist targeting in the M&A setting. To ensure a comprehensive sample, we purchase data for the period 2007 to 2015 from MyLogIQ, which provides compensation information for all public companies (instead of the 2,000 or so firms covered by Execucomp), and combine this data set with Execucomp data for the earlier 2000 to 2006 period. We then construct the dummy variableExcesscompensationby taking the residuals from a regression of CEO compensation on market value, book-to-market ratio, return on assets, and industry-year fixed effects and then coding the variable as equal to 1 if the residual is in the top quartile in the given year. The results, reported in Panel B of TableIV, show that M&A targets with “excessively” compensated CEOs are 48% to 52% more likely to trigger activism (an incremental probability of 3.5 to 3.7 percentage points).
The above evidence suggests that activist arbitrageurs serve as a governance remedy for outside shareholders, but they also extract rents from bidders in the sense that improved terms for target companies come at the expense of the acquirers. A priori it is not clear whether activists simply pursue “fair value,” or whether they try to push prices beyond what is fair for both sides. While “fair value” is notoriously difficult to measure, we nevertheless attempt to shed light on this question in the next set of tests.
The first test builds on the M&A literature that relates low acquirer returns to the degree of overpayment (Moeller, Schlingemann, and Stulz (2005), Masulius, Wang, and Xie (2007)). Focusing on the subsample of 92 deals in which the acquirers are also public (42% of the SDC-matched sample), we examine acquirer returns both around the announcement window (before an activist emerges) and over the period between the day proceeding the announcement and deal resolution (incorporating market reactions to activism). We find that the average abnormal acquirer return during the [−1, +2] window around the announcement of deals later targeted by activists is 0.5% (not significantly different from zero), while the average for a matched sample of nonevent deals is−1.08%(significantly different from zero at the 5% level), and the average abnormal acquirer return during the [−1, deal resolution] window for activist-involved deals is 0.11% (not significant), while the average for nonactivist deals is−4.52%(significant at the 5% level and consistent with the literature). The differences are not significant, and the sign and magnitude of the return comparisons do not support the view that activism leaves acquirers significantly worse off relative to similar deals in the M&A market, both ex ante and ex post.
In the second test, we analyze the subset of 33 activist-challenged deals that were later subject to appraisal litigation in the Delaware Court of Chancery and that used the judicially determined value as a basis for “fair value.” We observe the final valuation of only five cases that were actually ruled on by a judge (the rest were settled, presumably at prices that were acceptable to the activists15). Of the five deals, all received judicial valuation that was higher than the bid, with the average price revision by the court relative to the bid equal to 9.5%. Again, we find no evidence that activism renders acquirers a clearly unfair outcome.
article.III. Deal Resolution: Completion Rates,:
Whether arbitrageurs' campaigns increase deal risk reflects a curious trade-off. On the one hand, these sophisticated investors can push a target's board to reject inadequate offers and seek higher bids. On the other hand, activist arbitrageur involvement could cause delays due to extended negotiations or even withdrawals if the expectation of a higher premium drives potential suitors away. “Deal completion” can be measured in two ways in this context. We first classify a deal as “completed” if the target is sold to the buyer pursuant to the announcement, including under altered terms. Alternatively, we classify a deal as “completed” if the target is eventually sold to any buyer within one year from the original announcement. The first approach assesses the extent to which activists are associated with the uprooting of the original deal, while the alternative measure accounts for the fact that target shareholders could also benefit from a takeover by a different acquirer.
Panel A of TableVreports results of probit regressions of deal completion (using the two definitions) using activist involvement as the key independent variable. The results show that, other things being equal, deals targeted by activist arbitrageurs are 5.8 percentage points less likely to be completed with the current buyer, relative to the full-sample completion rate of 83.3% (significant at the 5% level). This difference is close to that reported in Panel B of TableII(without controls), suggesting that activists target deals that have a similar predicted probability of completion based on observable characteristics (in the absence of targeting). That is, some attributes that both attract activists and predict lower completion probability, such as going-private transactions and low-premium deals, offset other attributes that predict higher completion, such as friendly deals. Under the alternative definition of deal completion (i.e., by any buyer), the marginal effect is somewhat lower, at −4.6 percentage points, reflecting activists' tendency to put targets “in play” before selling them to an alternative acquirer. Consistent with existing literature, we also find that tender offers (Betton, Eckbo, and Thorburn (2008)) are more likely to be completed and that the use of defense tactics is associated with lower deal success rates (Field and Karpoff (2002)).
While attempting to affect deal completion, activist campaigns can cause delays in the merger process, imposing higher costs on shareholders even conditional on deal completion. To assess the extent of such a cost, in Panel B of TableVwe report results connecting the duration of completed deals to activist involvement. In columns (1) and (3), the dependent variable is the logarithm of the number of days between deal announcement and completion. In column (3), the dependent variable is modified to include the 129 deals for which the original agreement was withdrawn but the targets were successfully sold within one year to a third party. The key independent variable is the dummy variableActivist arbitrage; all other covariates are identical to those in Panel A. We find that a deal involving activists takes on average 7.2% to 10.2% longer (which amounts to 10 to 14 days for the average deal) than those without. The difference is statistically significant at the 10% level. The effects of the covariates are intuitive. On average, larger deals, stock mergers, and deals that involve defense tactics take longer to complete, while friendly bids, tender offers, and both high institutional ownership and insider ownership contribute to a shorter duration.
The Cox (1972) proportional hazards model,16reported in columns (2) and (4) of Panel B, TableV, yields qualitatively similar results. The estimated hazard ratio (which is equal to the exponentiated coefficient) associated with the dummy variableActivist arbitrageimplies that, conditional on a deal being in process, the probability of a current (any) deal closure on a given day is about 10.7% (17.5%) lower if the deal attracts an activist arbitrageur. The coefficient estimates are not consistently significant. Overall, the involvement of activists is marginally associated (both economically and statistically) with delays to closure.
Activists' ultimate goal is an upward revision in the deal price. Their intervention is associated with a positive price revision in 33.0% of deals, compared to 9.4% of deals without public dissension. TableV, Panel C, reports results of a regression that relatesPremium revisionto activist presence. Unconditionally, we find that price revision is 3.4 percentage points higher (significant at the 1% level) with activist intervention, controlling for deal characteristics and taking into account the fact that activism is also associated with an increased probability of deal failure. Conditional on deal completion, activism is associated with an incremental 4.3 percentage point increase in the premium paid to target shareholders.
One might argue that the presence of activists, if detectable at deal announcement, might affect the initial offered premium because acquirers anticipate pushback. To assess this possibility, we regress the deal premium on the aggregate holdings of all potential activists (in our sample) using information from the 13F filings and the same control variables as in TableV, Panel C. The results, reported in Table IAIV of the Internet Appendix, indicate that a one-percentage-point increase in activist holdings at the previous quarter-end (the average and standard deviation are 5.2% and 6.8%, respectively) is associated with a 13 basis point lower announcement premium, but the effect is not statistically significant.
Role of blockholders. The relationship between activist blockholders and deal resolution (both completion rate and premium revision) contrasts with the findings of two recent papers. First, Fich, Harford, and Tran (2015) document that the presence of “motivated” long-term institutional investors is associated with a lower probability of receiving a bid but ahigherprobability of completion—consistent with higher quality deals. However, Table IA.VI of the Internet Appendix shows that Fich, Harford, and Tran's (2015) measure of “motivated monitors” does not predict activist arbitrageurs, which suggests that the monitoring role of incumbent shareholders is distinct from that of specialized investors who become significant shareholders to pursue potential arbitrage opportunities.
Second, Boyson, Gantchev, and Shivdasani (2017) analyze a strategy whereby activists target firms that are “sellable” at a favorable price and facilitate such a transaction in which case activist presence is associated with more completed deals. Here, in addition to predicting the opposite relationship between deal completion and activist involvement, there is a critical difference between the two strategies in terms of activists' objectives and entrance point. In Boyson, Gantchev, and Shivdasani's (2017) setting, activists initiate or facilitate deals and therefore establish a toehold and announce their intention before deal announcement. In our setting, in contrast, activists emerge to work against an announced deal, as indicated by the timeline of Schedule 13D filings displayed in TableI. Hence, the effects of activists studied here are not driven by arbitrageurs' speculation on future, unannounced deals.17
While activists, as risk arbitrageurs, profit from deal completion and thus have an incentive to select deals with the best prospects of completion, their “treatment effects” are a priori unclear because the endogeneity in target selection may work in both directions. On the one hand, activists should target deals that are “inadequate” to start with and hence leave room for improvement. But the inadequacy of deal terms could be correlated with a high rate of failure due to other factors such as general shareholder resistance. On the other hand, because they benefit more from deal completion than deal failures, activists should target deals in which M&A targets (acquirers) are eager to sell (buy) and hence are more likely to yield to the activists' requests than to withdraw.
Overall, activists who pick targets randomly might not have much of an impact. Indeed, activists should concentrate their efforts where their marginal contribution to target value is expected to be greatest. The economics of “jaw-boning” suggest that the costly effort exerted by activists cannot be sustained in a rational equilibrium if the same outcome would have prevailed had the activists merely picked stocks and remained silent. Although the cost of activist arbitrage is difficult to estimate with accuracy, we nevertheless conduct a calibration based on the publicized costs of proxy access, including proxy solicitation fees and proxy contest costs.
In our sample, SharkRepellent reports solicitor fees and/or estimated proxy contest costs for 17 deals. The average (median) total cost of a proxy contest, including solicitor fees, is $960,000 ($450,000), while the average (median) solicitor fee is $410,000 ($140,000). We regress these costs on deal size and activist stake size to obtain predicted costs for solicitation and contest, respectively. The average (median) cost of activism for the full sample is estimated to be $1.1 million for activism that escalates to proxy contests and $260,000 for the rest. In SectionIV, we examine the extent to which such costs might overwhelm the returns from the arbitrage strategy. In the meanwhile, one can argue that, in a rational equilibrium, the prevalence as well as magnitude of premium revision would not have prevailed had it not been for the persistence of the activists, because otherwise the activists would act like passive investors conditional on deal selection.
Unlike in most other empirical settings, in ours we are able to observe, to a large extent, the dynamic development of events that are informative regarding the roles activists play in shaping the outcomes of announced M&A deals. In this section, we collect information (mostly manually) from a full set of SEC filings associated with the deals as well as from news archives about what activists did, and whether and how their actions likely affected the outcomes in the deals they select.
We say that “the activist likely contributes to deal failure” if at least one of the following is true: (1) the activist's vote is pivotal in a negative voting outcome, (2) the voting outcome is negative after ISS issues a recommendation that supports the activist, (3) management withdraws the deal, citing activist dissent, and there is no other concurrent public campaign against the deal, or (4) the firm is sold to an activist-recommended third party.
We classify a deal failure as “possibly due to general lack of support from target and/or acquirer” if the activist campaign coexists with at least one of the following: (1) there is a negative voting outcome following the activist's public campaign but the activist's vote was nonpivotal; (2) withdrawal by the target/acquirer occurs, citing lack of shareholder support, and there are concurrent public campaigns (in addition to the one led by the activist) against the deal; or (3) the firm is sold to a third party that is not affiliated with the activist.
Finally, we classify a deal failure as due to “external reasons” if at least one of the following is true: (1) the deal is canceled due to a “material adverse change,” or (2) the deal is canceled due to antitrust or other regulatory challenges.
Column (1) of TableVIreports the detailed classifications under the three broad categories for a total of 64 failed deals with activist presence. The sample is smaller than the full 277-deal sample as the analysis here is based on corporate filings and news instead of data from SDC. Note that the three categories are, but the subcategories are not, mutually exclusive. We find that, in 67.2% (column (1b)) of the failed deals with activist presence, activism appears to play a role.
In a placebo test, we collect a matching sample of deals involving no arbitrageurs, where a match is a deal announced in the same year as the activist-involved deal of the closest size in the second and third categories (since the first category is nonapplicable in the absence of an activist). This algorithm results in 48 matching deals, excluding duplications. Column (2) of TableVIreports the classifications of deals paralleling those in column (1). A comparison of the two columns highlights the potential role of activists in shaping the deals. First, deal failures due to material adverse changes (category #3.A) are comparable in the two samples (2.9% versus 2.4%), as presumably such changes are exogenous and hard to predict. However, activist risk arbitrageurs are less likely to target deals facing antitrust or other regulatory challenges (0.7% vs. 3.4%). Second, activist-targeted deals are less likely to fail when the activist role is nonpivotal (4.0% vs. 10.7%). Taken together, the evidence supports the view that activists select deals withlowerdeal failure risk in their absence.
article.IV. Returns from Activist Arbitrage:
Given the costs involved with activism, activist risk arbitrageurs must earn superior returns from their investments for such a strategy to be sustainable. In this section we empirically quantify the returns to activist arbitrage using “CARs,” sums of daily excess returns after adjusting for the Fama-French (1993) plus momentum factors, with an estimation window from 255 days to 54 days prior to deal announcement. Due to the unavailability of return information, the event sample for this analysis consists of 255 activist-involved deals that can be matched to CRSP.
In this section, we consider returns to investors who hold long positions in target companies over various time windows, following prior literature (Schwert (1996,2000), Gaspar, Massa, and Matos (2005)). TableVII, Panel A, presents the results.
TheCARover the full [–54, resolution] window (where resolution constitutes effective deal completion or withdrawal) equals the total takeover premium, which includes the well-documented “run-up” in M&A target companies' stock prices. The average total takeover premium involving activist arbitrageurs is 23.7%, which is significantly lower (at the 1% level) than the average of 31.7% (31.0%) for deals targeted by passive arbitrageurs (involving no disclosed arbitrageurs). The differences heavily concentrate in the “markup” period ([–1, resolution]) as opposed to the “run-up” period ([–54,–1]). Thus, activist arbitrageurs do not appear to rely on superior private information (whether through sophisticated takeover prediction models or insider information) prior to deal announcements.18Moreover, the difference in “markup” is driven completely by the “market premium” ([–1,+2]), confirming our earlier results that activists target deals with a lower initial premium.
To examine whether activist arbitrageurs earn superior abnormal returns for themselves, we next examine [+2, resolution]CARs, which do not include market reactions to merger announcements, to gauge returns from campaigning against a deal under the currently stated terms. The averageCAR(including failed deals) is 4.9% for deals involving activist arbitrageurs, which is greater than the average of 2.2% for deals targeted by passive arbitrageurs and of –2.0% for deals involving no disclosed arbitrageurs. However, only the latter difference is significant (at the 1% level).19In dollar terms, the average alpha implies a dollar net profit per deal of $3.9 million ($2.7 million) adjusted for the market factor (the Fama-French plus momentum factors), for a total net profit of $62.0 million ($42.7 million) in a given year.
In additional analysis, we calculateCARs over time windows that match activists' actual investment horizons by setting the starting time as max(+2, disclosure−10)—the later of the second trading day after the proposed deal is announced or 10 (calendar) days before an activist arbitrageur's disclosure of a large equity stake. The second term in the max function reflects the securities law that allows 10 days between when an investor crosses the 5% ownership threshold and when the investor must file a Schedule 13D.20We find that the averageCARduring the [max(+2, disclosure−10), resolution] window is 5.4%, which is significantly higher than the corresponding figures for the passive-only arbitrage (at the 10% level) and the no-arbitrage (at the 1% level) subsamples.
For completeness, in TableVIIwe also present short-term target stock returns around the emergence of activist arbitrageurs. Using a 20-day window around the disclosure dates, the average and medianCARs are 2.4% and 1.3%, respectively (both are significant at the 1% level). Thus, the market revises up the total premium expected upon learning of the arrival of activist arbitrageurs.
For stock/hybrid deals, conventional risk arbitrageurs often simultaneously take a long position in a target and a short position in the acquirer with the ratio of the long-short positions calibrated to the stock exchange ratio. When deploying such a strategy, an arbitrageur locks in the spread and profits from its full convergence if the deal goes through as planned. This strategy is potentially even better for activists because improving deal terms for the target comes at the cost of the acquirers.
It turns out that relatively few stock deals (48, or 18.8% of the activist subsample) are targeted by activist arbitrageurs, while about 31.8% of deals without activist arbitrageurs are stock deals. Panel B of TableVIIreports the long-short portfolio returns for the same time windows as in Panel A. Prior research documents that long-short abnormal returns are typically higher than long-only returns because acquirers' stock prices tend to decrease after deal announcement (Mitchell, Pulvino, and Stafford (2004)). The same pattern holds for activist arbitrageurs: their long-shortCARfor the [+2, resolution] window (8.1%) is higher than that of passive arbitrageurs (6.2%) or of deals with no disclosed arbitrageurs (–6.4%), but only the difference with the latter is significant (at the 10% level).
An alternative method used in the literature on long-term event studies is based on calendar-time portfolios. This approach has also become standard in analyzing the returns to shareholder activism (e.g., Brav et al. (2008b), Becht et al. (2017)). Accordingly, we next construct a portfolio of firms for which the event of interest occurred (the emergence of activist arbitrageurs) and define the abnormal return earned by this portfolio as the portfolio's excess return, or alpha, after adjusting for the market, size, value, and momentum factors. To facilitate comparison between activist and passive risk arbitrage, we closely follow the procedures described by Cao et al. (2016), who adopt the calendar-time portfolio approach to analyze the skills of hedge fund managers engaged in (passive) risk arbitrage.
More specifically, the calendar-time portfolio is rebalanced to allow new target firms to enter a portfolio two days after the announcement of an M&A deal and to leave the portfolio upon deal resolution (completed or withdrawn). Although returns are recorded at the monthly frequency, the first and last months of a firm may be incomplete, with the partial monthly returns derived from daily returns as specified by the time frame.21We therefore construct the time series of the activist arbitrage portfolio using weights based on the value of activists' holdings and the time series of the passive arbitrage portfolio using weights based on the value of incremental holdings of arbitrageurs over their holdings recorded at the quarter-end prior to deal announcement. For cash deals, the return corresponds to the return of the target, and for stock deals, the return corresponds to the long-short strategy specified in Section IV.B. Finally, we regress the monthly returns of the two portfolios on the common risk factors to obtain alpha as well asβMKT,βSMB,βHML, andβMOM. The results are reported in TableVIII.
Because this method involves fewer estimated parameters as inputs, the calendar-time portfolio approach tends to provide stronger statistical power compared with the tests reported in TableVII. Moreover, this method utilizes all 277 activist-involved deals in our sample to construct the activist risk arbitrage portfolio. We find that the activist arbitrage strategy produces a monthly four-factor alpha of 1.60%, significant at the 1% level. By comparison, the alpha of the passive arbitrage strategy is 0.51%, significant at the 1% level. The difference is significant at the 5% level. None of the loadings on the risk factors are statistically significant for the activist strategy.
While the results that we report thus far have been supportive, two more questions need to be addressed before we can conclude that activists are indeed adequately compensated for their additional “jaw pain.” First, it is unclear whether the positive alpha generated by the strategy will survive the costs involved with running activist campaigns. If we transform the alpha into dollar terms and subtract the estimated costs of activism as calibrated in Section IV.B.1, then the market-factor (four-factor) adjusted average net profits per deal is $3.5 million ($2.6 million).
Second, it is worth noting that the analysis of the calendar-time passive-strategy portfolio is already inclusive of the deals supported by passive arbitrageurs that were targeted by the activists. In comparison, our analysis of the passive-only risk arbitrage strategy as seen in TableVIIabstracts from the fact that, in reality, the passive arbitrageurs occasionally (with a probability of 6.2%) enjoy a “free ride” on activist effort if they happen to invest in deals that were later targeted by activists.22
In our analysis, we confirm that the abnormal returns earned by the activist arbitrage strategy are not driven by the fact that activists target deals with characteristics that are different from those of deals without activist involvement. TableIXreports results from regressions of various measures of returns on dummy variables indicating the presence of activist and passive arbitrageurs and on all the characteristics variables listed in TableIII. Since this analysis requires both return information (in the estimation ofCAR) and SDC information, the total sample drops from 3,374 deals (as in TableIII) to 3,352 deals and the sample activist deals drops from 220 deals (as in TableIII) to 218 deals.
The coefficients associated with both strategies are uniformly positive whether we use deal-level returns or normalized daily returns, and whether we use raw returns, returns in excess of the market, or returns adjusted for the Fama-French (1993) plus momentum factors. More importantly, the incremental return associated with activist arbitrage is consistently significant (at the 1% to 10% level) while that associated with the passive strategy is not. The last row of the table shows that the difference between the two coefficients is positive and significant in four out of the six specifications.
article.V. Conclusion:
In this paper, we provide the first study of the relatively new phenomenon of “activist risk arbitrage,” whereby activist shareholders wield their influence over corporate control changes by blending shareholder activism with an M&A arbitrage strategy. More specifically, activist arbitrageurs attempt to block announced M&A deals for which the premiums are perceived to be too low, or extract more favorable terms using public campaigns. They focus on deals susceptible to managerial conflicts of interest, including going-private deals (especially management buyouts), “friendly” deals (in which the boards endorse preferred buyers), and deals in which senior executives benefit disproportionately relative to outside shareholders via deal-specific severance terms. Conditional on the deals they select, activists appear to play a role in the decrease in probability that deals will be completed with the current bidders and the increase in premiums paid on completed deals as well as premiums averaged over all deals. Finally, activist risk arbitrages earn abnormal returns that are generally higher than those observed by passive arbitrage. Overall, the evidence suggests that activist risk arbitrage is a governance remedy for shareholders of target companies undergoing control changes and a viable investment strategy for activists themselves.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
